heroOverview: Isosporiasis is an intestinal protozoal infection caused by Cystoisospora belli (formerly Isospora belli), an apicomplexan coccidian parasite exclusive to humans with no known animal reservoir. The organism produces oocysts that are excreted in faeces and sporulate in the environment within 1-2 days to become infective, meaning direct person-to-person transmission is rarely immediate. Infection is acquired by swallowing sporulated oocysts in contaminated food or water in tropical and subtropical regions, particularly in parts of Latin America, sub-Saharan Africa, and Southeast Asia. In immunocompetent hosts, isosporiasis causes a self-limiting watery diarrhoeal illness lasting 1-3 weeks with prominent eosinophilia — a distinguishing feature among intestinal protozoal infections. In people living with advanced HIV/AIDS, it causes profuse chronic watery diarrhoea that can persist for months and contributes to wasting and death without treatment. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is highly effective and represents the treatment of choice, making isosporiasis unusual among AIDS-related opportunistic infections in having a reliably curative antibiotic.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated prevalence of 0.2-3% in general populations in endemic tropical regions; up to 15-20% in people with AIDS in some sub-Saharan African cohort studies
keyStats.demographics: Highest burden in HIV-positive individuals with advanced immunosuppression (CD4 < 200 cells/µL) in tropical countries; also affects immunocompetent travellers and children in endemic areas
keyStats.avgAge: Affects all ages; clinical disease in immunocompetent hosts most commonly reported in young adults and travellers; severe disease concentrated in adults with advanced HIV
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global incidence unknown due to limited surveillance; historically one of the most common intestinal coccidian infections in people with AIDS before widespread ART availability

definition: Isosporiasis (ICD-10: A07.3) is an infection of the small intestinal epithelium by Cystoisospora belli, a coccidian apicomplexan parasite. The organism's life cycle is monoxenous (occurring in a single host — humans only). Sporulated oocysts (20-33 µm × 10-16 µm, distinctively elongated and ellipsoidal) are ingested with contaminated food or water; sporozoites are released in the duodenum, invade enterocytes of the proximal small intestine, and undergo intracellular asexual multiplication (merogony) and sexual multiplication (gametogony) that produces new oocysts. Unlike Cryptosporidium, C. belli is an intracellular parasite that occupies the cytoplasm rather than an extracytoplasmic niche, and its larger oocysts are easily identified on acid-fast staining. Pathogenesis involves villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and inflammatory infiltration of the lamina propria with eosinophils — the last feature being diagnostically distinctive because most other intestinal protozoan infections do not cause peripheral eosinophilia. Malabsorption of fat, xylose, and vitamin B12 occurs in prolonged cases. Extraintestinal dissemination to lymph nodes, liver, spleen, and lung has been described rarely in profoundly immunocompromised patients. Isosporiasis is classified as an AIDS-defining opportunistic infection by the WHO and CDC, and the infection is managed within the clinical domains of infectious disease, HIV medicine, and tropical medicine.

typesClassification.description: A self-limiting febrile diarrhoeal illness typically lasting 2-3 weeks, with watery non-bloody diarrhoea, abdominal cramps, and weight loss. Peripheral eosinophilia is often present. Resolution without specific treatment occurs in most cases.

Chronic, debilitating watery diarrhoea producing large-volume stools for months without treatment. Associated with malabsorption, weight loss, wasting, and electrolyte disturbances. CD4 counts below 200 cells/µL substantially increase risk of chronic disease.

Acute illness in travellers returning from endemic tropical regions; clinically resembles other causes of traveller's diarrhoea but distinguished by eosinophilia and the characteristic elongated oocysts on stool acid-fast staining.
Exceptionally rare; documented in patients with profound immunosuppression where the parasite disseminates to mesenteric lymph nodes, spleen, and other organs. Associated with high mortality.

primarySymptoms: Profuse watery diarrhoea — typically non-bloody, pale, and foul-smelling — occurring 4-10 times daily and reflecting small intestinal malabsorption rather than colonic inflammation.

Abdominal cramps and colicky pain, predominantly in the periumbilical and lower abdominal regions, coinciding with diarrhoeal episodes and caused by abnormal intestinal motility from enterocyte damage.
Nausea and vomiting, more prominent early in the illness, reducing oral intake and compounding fluid losses from diarrhoea.
Fever and chills, present in a majority of immunocompetent patients early in the illness; fever may be absent in chronic AIDS-associated disease.
Weight loss and malnutrition from malabsorption of fats, carbohydrates, and micronutrients, particularly pronounced in prolonged infection and in immunocompromised patients.
Anorexia and profound fatigue arising from systemic inflammatory response and reduced caloric intake; fatigue may outlast the acute diarrhoeal phase.
Headache accompanying the febrile phase of illness in immunocompetent patients during the first week.
Steatorrhoea — fatty, greasy, pale stools that float and are difficult to flush — indicating fat malabsorption from small bowel villous damage in prolonged cases.

Peripheral eosinophilia — an elevation in circulating eosinophils detected on full blood count — a distinctive laboratory feature of isosporiasis not typically seen in other intestinal protozoan infections.
earlyWarningSigns: Watery diarrhoea beginning 1-2 weeks after travel to a tropical endemic area without an obvious bacterial cause.
Mild fever with increasing stool frequency over 2-3 days, particularly in a traveller or in a person with known HIV.
Unexplained peripheral eosinophilia on routine blood count in a patient with chronic diarrhoea.
Persistent diarrhoea lasting more than 2 weeks despite dietary modifications and symptomatic treatment.
emergencySigns: Signs of severe dehydration — very dry mouth, no urine for 8 hours, dizziness, rapid pulse — requiring urgent intravenous rehydration.
Profound wasting with inability to maintain oral intake in an HIV-positive patient, indicating severe malabsorption requiring parenteral nutrition alongside specific treatment.
New fever, confusion, or jaundice in an immunocompromised patient with diarrhoea, suggesting disseminated infection or concurrent opportunistic illness.
Bloody stools developing during an established isosporiasis illness, suggesting superimposed bacterial infection or intestinal complication.

causes.cause: Ingestion of sporulated Cystoisospora belli oocysts in contaminated food
causes.description: Food contaminated with human faeces — particularly raw produce, unwashed fruits, and food prepared by infected food handlers without adequate hygiene — is the most common transmission route. Oocysts require 1-2 days of environmental sporulation to become infective, so immediate person-to-person transmission is less common than with Cryptosporidium.

causes.cause: Drinking contaminated water in endemic regions
causes.description: Water supplies contaminated with human sewage in tropical and subtropical countries deliver sporulated oocysts to unsuspecting consumers. Oocysts are moderately resistant to chlorination but are killed by boiling. A relatively higher infective dose is required than for Cryptosporidium, though the minimum infective dose in humans has not been precisely established.

causes.cause: Poor sanitation and faeco-oral transmission via contaminated environments
causes.description: In settings without adequate sewage treatment, toilet facilities, or handwashing infrastructure, oocysts deposited in soil and on surfaces can be picked up via contaminated hands and subsequently ingested. Transmission is particularly efficient in crowded settings with shared latrine facilities.

causes.cause: Immunosuppression allowing chronic or recurrent infection
causes.description: In immunocompetent individuals, the infection self-limits within weeks as CD4 T-cells and IgA mount an effective mucosal immune response. In individuals with HIV and CD4 counts below 200 cells/µL, especially below 50 cells/µL, this immune control fails, allowing sustained intracellular replication and continuous high-volume oocyst output.

Travellers from high-income countries visiting tropical regions in Latin America, sub-Saharan Africa, and Southeast Asia encounter C. belli in food and water that local populations are often semi-immune to; acute traveller's isosporiasis occurs when a naive host ingests sufficient sporulated oocysts from local food or water sources.

riskFactors.description: Isosporiasis is a CDC and WHO AIDS-defining opportunistic infection; the risk of chronic debilitating isosporiasis rises dramatically as CD4 counts fall below 200 cells/µL. In pre-ART era cohorts in sub-Saharan Africa, isosporiasis was found in 15-20% of AIDS patients with chronic diarrhoea.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to tropical and subtropical endemic regions
riskFactors.description: The parasite is endemic in parts of Latin America (especially Haiti, Brazil), sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific Islands; travellers from non-endemic countries to these regions are susceptible because they lack any acquired immunity to C. belli.

Malnourished individuals — particularly children in high-burden settings — have impaired mucosal immunity and are more likely to develop prolonged illness from a C. belli exposure that would be self-limiting in a well-nourished host.

riskFactors.factor: Other immunosuppressive conditions (organ transplant, haematological malignancy)
riskFactors.description: Solid organ transplant recipients on immunosuppressive regimens and patients receiving corticosteroid or chemotherapy for haematological cancers are at elevated risk of severe and prolonged isosporiasis; case reports confirm severe isosporiasis in non-HIV immunosuppression.

riskFactors.factor: Inadequate sanitation and unsafe water access
riskFactors.description: Communities relying on pit latrines, open defecation, or untreated surface water create the environmental conditions that sustain C. belli transmission; the parasite's requirement for sporulation in the environment means it persists well in humid tropical soils.

riskFactors.factor: Poor hand hygiene and food handling practices
riskFactors.description: Food handlers with isosporiasis who do not practice thorough handwashing can deposit oocysts onto food during preparation; sporulation occurs before the food is consumed, completing transmission. This is a documented mechanism in institutional and restaurant outbreaks.

Co-infection with Cryptosporidium, Giardia, or intestinal helminths is common in the same environments where C. belli thrives, and helminth-induced immunomodulation may facilitate C. belli persistence in some populations.

affectedDemographics: HIV-positive adults in tropical countries with CD4 counts below 200 cells/µL
Travellers from high-income countries visiting endemic tropical regions
Malnourished children in sub-Saharan Africa, Latin America, and Southeast Asia
Immunocompromised individuals regardless of HIV status, including transplant recipients
Residents of communities with limited sanitation and open-water sources in endemic regions
People living or working in institutional settings (prisons, camps) with shared latrine facilities in endemic areas
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of isosporiasis rests on demonstration of C. belli oocysts in stool specimens using acid-fast staining. The oocysts are large (20-33 µm long, 10-19 µm wide), distinctly ellipsoidal, and stain pinkish-red with modified Ziehl-Neelsen or Kinyoun methods — features that make them far easier to identify than the much smaller Cryptosporidium oocysts. However, oocyst excretion can be intermittent and sparse, particularly early in illness, so at least three stool samples collected on separate days should be examined if the first is negative. Concentration techniques (formalin-ethyl acetate sedimentation) significantly improve sensitivity. Autofluorescence under ultraviolet microscopy can also be used to identify oocysts. Duodenal aspiration or biopsy — showing villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and parasitic stages within enterocytes — may be diagnostic when stool tests are repeatedly negative in a patient with compatible illness; this is particularly relevant in immunocompromised patients with very low parasite burden. Peripheral blood eosinophilia (typically 5-15% eosinophils, sometimes higher) is a helpful diagnostic clue and is more consistently present in isosporiasis than in any other intestinal protozoan infection. Molecular (PCR) methods for C. belli detection are available at reference centres and some commercial multiplex panels, providing higher sensitivity for cases missed by microscopy. Differential diagnoses include cryptosporidiosis, cyclosporiasis, giardiasis, bacterial traveller's diarrhoea, and, in HIV-positive patients, cytomegalovirus colitis and microsporidia.

diagnosticTests.test: Modified acid-fast (Ziehl-Neelsen or Kinyoun) stained stool smear
diagnosticTests.purpose: Primary method for identifying C. belli oocysts; the large elongated oocysts (20-33 µm) stain red-pink on a blue background, easily distinguishable from Cryptosporidium by size alone.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Submit fresh or preserved stool in a specimen container; at least 3 samples on separate days improve sensitivity. The laboratory processes the specimen through a concentration step before staining; results available in 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool wet mount examination under autofluorescence UV microscopy
diagnosticTests.purpose: Oocysts autofluoresce blue-white under UV light, allowing identification without staining; a rapid complementary method useful in settings with access to UV microscopy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fresh stool smear on a glass slide is viewed under a fluorescence microscope; technique takes 10-15 minutes per sample.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count with differential (peripheral eosinophilia)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies eosinophilia (typically > 5% eosinophils on differential), a hallmark of isosporiasis and helpful distinguishing feature from other intestinal protozoan infections where eosinophilia is absent.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard arm blood draw; results available within a few hours. Eosinophil percentage is reported automatically on a standard differential count.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive diagnosis when stool testing is repeatedly negative; villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and intracellular parasitic stages within enterocytes confirm infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy under sedation; a small biopsy is taken from the duodenum and sent to a histopathology laboratory. Results take 2-5 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Highest-sensitivity molecular detection, particularly in immunocompromised patients with sparse oocyst excretion; available on some multiplex gastrointestinal panels and reference laboratories.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Stool in a preservative or molecular transport buffer sent to a reference or molecular laboratory; results available in 1-5 working days depending on the testing platform.

diagnosticTests.test: CD4 T-cell count (in HIV-positive or immunocompromised patients)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes immune status to guide treatment intensity and the need for secondary prophylaxis; CD4 below 200 cells/µL indicates AIDS-defining disease requiring suppressive therapy after initial cure.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw processed by an immunology or virology laboratory; results typically within 24-48 hours.

treatmentOverview:
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX, co-trimoxazole) is the treatment of choice for isosporiasis and is highly effective in both immunocompetent and immunocompromised patients — a distinguishing advantage over cryptosporidiosis, for which no consistently reliable treatment exists in immunosuppression. The standard regimen for acute isosporiasis in adults is TMP-SMX 160/800 mg (one double-strength tablet) twice daily for 7-10 days in immunocompetent patients, extended to 3-4 weeks in immunocompromised individuals to prevent relapse. Symptomatic response is typically rapid — diarrhoea volume and frequency often halve within 48-72 hours of starting treatment.

For HIV-positive patients, secondary prophylaxis with TMP-SMX 160/800 mg once daily three times per week (or daily) is recommended after completion of the acute course to prevent relapse, which occurs in approximately 50% of patients without prophylaxis. Prophylaxis can be discontinued when CD4 counts rise above 200 cells/µL and are sustained on effective antiretroviral therapy. Pyrimethamine (75 mg daily) with folinic acid is an effective alternative for patients who cannot tolerate sulfonamides. Ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 7 days has also demonstrated efficacy in small studies. Oral rehydration is essential for all patients throughout the diarrhoeal phase. Nutritional rehabilitation is a parallel priority in malnourished individuals. In HIV-positive patients, initiation or optimisation of antiretroviral therapy to achieve immune reconstitution is the definitive long-term strategy for preventing recurrence and is the reason isosporiasis incidence has declined sharply in the ART era.

medicalTreatments.name: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX, co-trimoxazole)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for all forms of isosporiasis. Standard adult dose is 160 mg TMP / 800 mg SMX (one double-strength tablet) orally twice daily. Immunocompetent adults: 7-10 days. Immunocompromised adults and HIV-positive patients: 3-4 weeks acute course followed by secondary prophylaxis. Available as intravenous formulation for patients unable to take oral medications.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Parasitological and clinical cure rates above 90% within 7-14 days in both immunocompetent and immunocompromised patients; symptomatic improvement typically within 48-72 hours of initiation. Relapse after treatment in HIV-positive patients without secondary prophylaxis occurs in approximately 50% within 6 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Secondary prophylaxis with TMP-SMX (for HIV-positive patients)
medicalTreatments.description: TMP-SMX 160/800 mg orally three times weekly (or daily) given after completion of the acute treatment course to prevent relapse in HIV-positive patients until CD4 count exceeds 200 cells/µL on effective ART. Also provides incidental prophylaxis against Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and toxoplasmosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Secondary prophylaxis with TMP-SMX reduces isosporiasis relapse from approximately 50% to less than 10% in the 6 months following treatment in HIV-positive patients; cessation is safe after sustained CD4 recovery above 200 cells/µL.

medicalTreatments.name: Pyrimethamine with folinic acid (sulfonamide-intolerant patients)
medicalTreatments.description: Pyrimethamine 75 mg daily orally with folinic acid 10 mg daily given to patients who cannot tolerate TMP-SMX due to allergy, haematological toxicity, or severe renal impairment. Folinic acid supplementation is essential to prevent pyrimethamine-induced megaloblastic anaemia and bone marrow suppression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates comparable to TMP-SMX in published case series — approximately 80-90% in 2 weeks; less extensively studied but widely used as second-line in HIV medicine.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral rehydration solution (ORS)
medicalTreatments.description: WHO/UNICEF oral rehydration solution replaces fluid, sodium, potassium, and glucose lost during diarrhoeal illness. Essential adjunct for all patients during the acute phase. At least 2 litres daily for adults with moderate diarrhoea; intravenous rehydration for severe dehydration.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents dehydration-related mortality; essential in any diarrhoeal illness regardless of aetiology. Reduces need for hospitalisation when started early and maintained throughout illness.

medicalTreatments.name: Antiretroviral therapy (ART) for HIV-associated isosporiasis
medicalTreatments.description: Immune reconstitution to CD4 counts above 200 cells/µL is the definitive long-term strategy for preventing isosporiasis relapse in HIV-positive patients. ART initiation should not be delayed when isosporiasis is diagnosed in ART-naive patients. After CD4 recovery, secondary TMP-SMX prophylaxis can be safely discontinued.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Sustained CD4 count above 200 cells/µL on ART reduces isosporiasis relapse risk to near zero in prospective cohort studies; ART-era incidence of AIDS-defining isosporiasis has fallen by more than 85% in countries with universal ART access.

alternativeTreatments.name: Ciprofloxacin (alternative treatment)
alternativeTreatments.description: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 7 days has been shown effective in small randomised studies for acute isosporiasis in immunocompetent patients; less favoured than TMP-SMX due to limited data in immunocompromised patients and antibiotic stewardship concerns.

alternativeTreatments.name: Nutritional rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: High-protein, calorie-dense dietary supplementation to reverse malnutrition in malnourished patients; critical adjunct because malnutrition both worsens isosporiasis outcomes and is worsened by malabsorption from the infection. Enteral or parenteral nutrition may be needed in severe cases.

alternativeTreatments.name: Safe water and food hygiene during travel
alternativeTreatments.description: Boiling water for at least 1 minute, using sealed bottled water, and avoiding raw produce that may be contaminated are effective primary prevention measures for travellers visiting endemic regions. C. belli oocysts are killed by heat and standard chlorination is less reliable.

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease or HIV/tropical medicine specialist should be consulted for all immunocompromised patients with isosporiasis, for cases not responding to TMP-SMX within 5 days, for patients with persistent eosinophilia after treatment suggesting incomplete cure, and for any patient in whom disseminated infection is suspected. Specialist involvement ensures the correct agent is used, secondary prophylaxis is appropriately prescribed, and underlying immunosuppression is managed alongside the infection.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a physician with training in infectious disease, HIV medicine, or tropical medicine who has access to a laboratory with acid-fast stool microscopy capability. In high-income countries where isosporiasis is rarely encountered, a travel medicine or tropical disease clinic will have the most relevant experience. For HIV-positive patients, a centre with HIV expertise and ART management capability is essential for long-term secondary prophylaxis planning and immune reconstitution.
preventionStrategies: Drink boiled or filtered water in all endemic regions and exercise caution with tap water, ice, and beverages prepared with local water.
Wash all raw vegetables and fruits thoroughly with clean water and peel them where possible before eating.
Wash hands with soap and water before eating and after using the toilet; alcohol gel does not reliably inactivate coccidian oocysts.
Maintain effective antiretroviral therapy in HIV-positive individuals to keep CD4 counts above 200 cells/µL — the most effective protection against severe isosporiasis.
For HIV-positive individuals with CD4 below 200 cells/µL not yet on ART, consider empirical TMP-SMX prophylaxis, which simultaneously prevents Pneumocystis pneumonia and toxoplasmosis.
For travellers to endemic regions, follow food and water hygiene precautions consistently throughout the visit.
Invest in sanitation improvements in endemic communities — proper sewage treatment, latrine provision, and hand hygiene promotion reduce transmission at the population level.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain consistent food and water hygiene practices in endemic settings even when asymptomatic — oocysts are invisible and odourless.
Complete the full TMP-SMX course regardless of symptom resolution; premature discontinuation is a common cause of relapse.
For HIV-positive patients: do not discontinue secondary prophylaxis until CD4 count is confirmed above 200 cells/µL on two consecutive measurements 3 months apart.
Use a diet low in fat during the acute illness phase if steatorrhoea is present, transitioning to a higher-calorie intake as symptoms resolve.
Avoid raw shellfish, salads, and street food in endemic regions during travel.
Monitor stool frequency and character during treatment and report persistence or worsening to the prescribing clinician.

recommended: Oral rehydration solution to replace fluid and electrolytes lost through diarrhoea
Bland, easily digestible foods (rice, boiled potatoes, bananas, plain boiled meat) during the acute phase
High-protein, energy-dense foods during recovery to address malabsorption-related nutritional deficits
Boiled or sealed bottled water in all endemic and travel settings
Cooked vegetables and peeled fruits to reduce oocyst ingestion risk
avoid: Raw salads, unpeeled fruit, and raw vegetables in endemic regions — common vehicles for oocyst transmission
Fatty and high-fibre foods during steatorrhoea phase, which worsen malabsorptive diarrhoea
Alcohol and caffeine-containing drinks during acute illness, which increase intestinal irritability
Tap water and ice drinks in regions with inadequate sanitation

exerciseGuidelines: Rest is recommended during the acute febrile diarrhoeal phase. Once fever resolves and diarrhoea frequency decreases substantially, gradual return to normal activity is appropriate. For immunocompromised patients recovering from severe isosporiasis with wasting, a supervised gradual return to activity coordinated with nutritional rehabilitation is advisable. There are no specific permanent exercise restrictions once infection is cleared.
dailyManagement: Take every dose of TMP-SMX at the prescribed times; set a phone reminder if needed — missed doses are the most common reason for incomplete cure.
Maintain fluid intake at a minimum of 2 litres of oral rehydration solution or clean water daily throughout diarrhoeal illness.
Log stool frequency and character daily during treatment to detect early response or treatment failure.
For HIV-positive patients, confirm with your HIV clinician that secondary prophylaxis has been prescribed after the acute course, and confirm when it is safe to discontinue.
Return for stool testing 2-3 weeks after completing treatment to confirm parasitological cure.
Notify household members and close contacts so they can be tested if they develop diarrhoea.
prognosis: The prognosis for isosporiasis in immunocompetent individuals is excellent: with appropriate TMP-SMX treatment, more than 90% achieve clinical and parasitological cure within 7-14 days, and relapses are uncommon. Without treatment, self-limited recovery occurs in the majority within 2-3 weeks, though the illness can occasionally persist for months. Post-infectious malabsorption may continue for several weeks after parasitological eradication due to intestinal villous atrophy, necessitating dietary attention. In HIV-positive patients treated with TMP-SMX, initial cure rates also exceed 90%, but relapse without secondary prophylaxis approaches 50% within 6 weeks. With effective secondary TMP-SMX prophylaxis and immune reconstitution on ART, relapse rates fall to below 10%, and long-term remission is achievable once CD4 counts exceed 200 cells/µL sustainably. Before the ART era, isosporiasis was a major AIDS-defining illness in tropical countries and contributed substantially to AIDS-related mortality through malabsorption and wasting. Access to ART has dramatically changed this outlook, making the prognosis for HIV-associated isosporiasis fundamentally dependent on the availability and adherence to antiretroviral therapy.
recoveryTimeline: In immunocompetent patients, diarrhoea volume and frequency typically halve within 48-72 hours of starting TMP-SMX, with near-complete resolution within 7-10 days. A full 10-day course should be completed. Minor ongoing changes in bowel habit from villous recovery may persist for 2-4 weeks. In immunocompromised patients, resolution of acute symptoms takes 2-4 weeks on extended treatment; secondary prophylaxis continues beyond this point. Nutritional recovery in malnourished patients requires several additional weeks of dietary rehabilitation.
complications: Chronic relapsing diarrhoea and wasting — without secondary prophylaxis in HIV-positive patients, relapse occurs in approximately 50% within 6 weeks of initial cure.
Malabsorption syndrome — villous atrophy from prolonged infection causes fat, vitamin B12, folate, and micronutrient malabsorption, contributing to anaemia and peripheral neuropathy.
Severe malnutrition and cachexia — in untreated or relapsing AIDS-related isosporiasis, profound wasting with BMI below 16 kg/m² is documented.
Disseminated infection — exceptionally rare; spread of C. belli to mesenteric lymph nodes, liver, and spleen in profoundly immunocompromised patients; associated with very high mortality.
Dehydration and electrolyte imbalances — particularly hyponatraemia and hypokalaemia from high-volume watery diarrhoea; requires active replacement.
Secondary anaemia — folate and vitamin B12 malabsorption from prolonged intestinal disease, compounded by TMP-SMX-induced folate antagonism without folinic acid supplementation.

Sorvillo FJ et al. Isospora belli infections among patients with the acquired immunodeficiency syndrome. Am J Trop Med Hyg, 1990

confusedWithConditions.name: Cryptosporidiosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cryptosporidiosis is caused by a much smaller oocyst (4-6 µm vs 20-33 µm for C. belli), does not cause peripheral eosinophilia, occurs in immunocompetent individuals more commonly than isosporiasis, and is not reliably treated by TMP-SMX — the key differentiating therapeutic feature.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cyclosporiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cyclosporiasis is caused by Cyclospora cayetanensis, with intermediate-sized oocysts (8-10 µm) that fluoresce under UV and are unsporulated when freshly shed (not immediately infective). Both respond to TMP-SMX and cause eosinophilia, but cyclosporiasis has distinct epidemiology linked to fresh produce outbreaks.

confusedWithConditions.name: Giardiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Giardia lamblia causes malabsorptive watery diarrhoea with sulphurous belching from small bowel colonisation, but it does not cause peripheral eosinophilia, the oocysts are not acid-fast, and the infection responds to metronidazole or tinidazole rather than TMP-SMX.

confusedWithConditions.name: Amebiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Entamoeba histolytica causes invasive colonic disease with bloody dysentery and potential liver abscess — not small bowel malabsorptive watery diarrhoea; eosinophilia is absent or mild; treated with metronidazole plus a luminal agent.

relatedConditions.name: Cryptosporidiosis
relatedConditions.relevance: The closest clinical parallel — both are apicomplexan coccidian parasites causing small bowel disease and profuse watery diarrhoea disproportionately severe in advanced HIV, and both are diagnosed by acid-fast stool microscopy.

relatedConditions.name: Cyclosporiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another coccidian parasite causing prolonged watery diarrhoea; shares TMP-SMX as first-line treatment and similar epidemiological settings with isosporiasis.

relatedConditions.name: Giardiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A common differential diagnosis for small bowel malabsorptive diarrhoea; co-endemic with isosporiasis in tropical settings and detected by the same stool examination panel.

relatedConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
relatedConditions.relevance: Isosporiasis is an AIDS-defining opportunistic infection; its severity and recurrence are directly determined by CD4 count, making HIV management central to long-term isosporiasis control.

faqs.question: What is isosporiasis and what causes it?
faqs.answer: Isosporiasis is an intestinal infection caused by Cystoisospora belli, a protozoan parasite found exclusively in humans. Infection occurs by swallowing sporulated oocysts in contaminated food or water. Unlike most intestinal parasites, it has no animal reservoir — it spreads only from humans. It is most common in tropical countries with poor sanitation.

faqs.question: How is isosporiasis different from cryptosporidiosis?
faqs.answer: Both are coccidian parasites causing watery diarrhoea, but they differ in important ways. Isosporiasis oocysts are much larger and easier to identify under a microscope. Isosporiasis typically causes peripheral eosinophilia (elevated eosinophils in the blood), which cryptosporidiosis does not. Isosporiasis responds well to trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole; cryptosporidiosis does not reliably.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of isosporiasis?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are watery, foul-smelling diarrhoea, abdominal cramps, nausea, and fever. The diarrhoea is typically non-bloody. Weight loss, fatigue, and a fatty appearance to stools (steatorrhoea) occur in prolonged cases. A blood test often shows elevated eosinophils — a characteristic finding that helps distinguish isosporiasis from other diarrhoeal illnesses.

faqs.question: How is isosporiasis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is made by finding C. belli oocysts in stool samples using modified acid-fast staining. The oocysts are large and distinctly elongated — easily visible under a microscope. Because oocyst excretion can be intermittent, three separate stool samples over different days improve the chance of detection. A blood count showing eosinophilia provides an additional diagnostic clue.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for isosporiasis?
faqs.answer: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX, co-trimoxazole) is the first-line treatment and is highly effective. In healthy people, a 7-10 day course typically clears the infection within 1-2 weeks. HIV-positive patients receive a longer 3-4 week course followed by ongoing preventive doses until their immune system recovers with antiretroviral therapy.

faqs.question: Is isosporiasis dangerous for people with HIV?
faqs.answer: Yes. In people with advanced HIV (CD4 count below 200 cells per microlitre), isosporiasis causes chronic, relentless watery diarrhoea that can last for months, leading to malnutrition and wasting. Without treatment and immune reconstitution via antiretroviral therapy, it is a life-threatening condition. It is classified as an AIDS-defining opportunistic infection by the WHO and CDC.

faqs.question: Can isosporiasis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Without ongoing preventive doses of trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, about 50% of HIV-positive patients relapse within 6 weeks of completing the initial treatment. This is why secondary prophylaxis — a lower preventive dose taken 3 times weekly — is continued until CD4 counts recover above 200 cells per microlitre on antiretroviral therapy.

faqs.question: Does isosporiasis cause a high eosinophil count?
faqs.answer: Yes. Peripheral blood eosinophilia — an elevated percentage of eosinophils on a full blood count — is characteristic of isosporiasis and is more consistently present than in any other common intestinal protozoan infection. Finding eosinophilia in a patient with watery diarrhoea from an endemic region should prompt stool examination for C. belli.

faqs.question: Who is most at risk of isosporiasis?
faqs.answer: The highest risk groups are HIV-positive people with low CD4 counts (below 200), travellers to tropical endemic countries, and malnourished children in sub-Saharan Africa, Latin America, and Southeast Asia. Immunocompromised individuals receiving organ transplant medications or chemotherapy are also at elevated risk of severe disease.

faqs.question: How can isosporiasis be prevented?
faqs.answer: Drink boiled or sealed bottled water in endemic regions. Wash hands with soap and water before eating and after using the toilet. Wash and peel raw vegetables and fruits. For HIV-positive individuals with low CD4 counts, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis provides effective protection. Antiretroviral therapy to keep the immune system strong is the most durable preventive measure in HIV.

faqs.question: Can isosporiasis spread from person to person?
faqs.answer: Direct person-to-person transmission is possible but requires a delay, because freshly shed oocysts need 1-2 days outside the body to become infective. This means immediate handshake-type contact rarely transmits the infection; it typically spreads via contaminated food and water consumed after oocysts have had time to sporulate.

faqs.question: Is isosporiasis the same as Isospora belli infection?
faqs.answer: Yes. The organism was previously called Isospora belli; it was reclassified and renamed Cystoisospora belli in 2005 based on molecular and morphological studies. Both names refer to the same parasite. Some older medical literature and some regional health systems still use the original Isospora belli name.

faqs.question: What happens if isosporiasis is not treated?
faqs.answer: In healthy people, isosporiasis usually self-resolves within 2-3 weeks even without specific treatment. However, in HIV-positive or otherwise immunocompromised individuals, untreated infection progresses to chronic unremitting diarrhoea, severe weight loss, malnutrition, and potentially death. For this group, prompt diagnosis and treatment are essential.

faqs.question: Is isosporiasis common in the United Kingdom and United States?
faqs.answer: Locally acquired isosporiasis is rare in both countries. Cases seen in the UK and US occur almost exclusively in HIV-positive individuals with advanced immunosuppression and in travellers returning from endemic tropical regions. Any HIV-positive patient with chronic watery diarrhoea should be tested, regardless of travel history.

faqs.question: How is isosporiasis different from cyclosporiasis?
faqs.answer: Both are coccidian protozoa causing watery diarrhoea that respond to TMP-SMX, but cyclosporiasis is caused by Cyclospora cayetanensis with smaller oocysts (8-10 µm compared to 20-33 µm for C. belli). Cyclospora oocysts autofluoresce under UV and are unsporulated when freshly passed, making them non-infective immediately. Cyclosporiasis also causes more prominent fatigue and tends to affect immunocompetent people via contaminated fresh produce outbreaks.

faqs.question: How long does it take for isosporiasis to respond to trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole?
faqs.answer: Most patients notice a significant reduction in diarrhoea frequency and volume within 48 to 72 hours of starting TMP-SMX. Near-complete resolution of diarrhoea occurs within 7-10 days in immunocompetent patients. HIV-positive patients on the extended 3-4 week course typically see improvement within 1 week, though full resolution may take 2-3 weeks given the greater intestinal damage.

faqs.question: Can isosporiasis cause vitamin B12 deficiency?
faqs.answer: Yes. Prolonged isosporiasis causes small bowel villous atrophy and malabsorption that can impair absorption of vitamin B12, folate, and fat-soluble vitamins. Vitamin B12 deficiency may contribute to fatigue, peripheral neuropathy, and megaloblastic anaemia in chronic cases. Supplementation should be considered in patients with prolonged disease or documented deficiency.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for diarrhoea that might be isosporiasis?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if you have watery diarrhoea lasting more than 1 week, especially after travel to a tropical country, or if you are HIV-positive and develop any new diarrhoea. Seek urgent care for signs of dehydration — very dry mouth, no urination for 8 hours, dizziness — and if you notice bloody stools, high fever, or confusion alongside the diarrhoea.

metaDescription: Isosporiasis is a coccidian parasite infection causing watery diarrhoea and eosinophilia. Learn symptoms, TMP-SMX treatment, HIV risk, and prevention tips.
